summary_text:
S:
The patient reports a sore right foot, with intermittent swelling and tenderness. He reports a previous ankle injury twenty years ago that occasionally causes soreness. He also reports soreness in his other ankle and has had surgery on that ankle. He describes the initial foot injury as dropping a landscape brick on his foot, causing immediate pain and swelling. He rated his current pain as eleven out of ten. He reports numbness in the entire foot since the incident. He has been taking no medications for the pain.

O:
Physical exam reveals bruising on the bottom and top of the right foot. Swelling is present. Capillary refill is brisk. Dorsalis pedis pulse is strong and bounding. Neurovascular exam is intact. X-ray shows dorsal displacement of the base of the second metatarsal with a three millimeter separation of the first and second metatarsal bases and presence of bony fragments.

A:
The patient's right foot pain is due to a Lisfranc fracture. The x-ray findings indicate a dorsal displacement of the second metatarsal and separation of the first and second metatarsals. Given the bony fragments, surgery is likely indicated to restore proper alignment and prevent long-term complications such as arch loss, flatfoot, and arthritis. An MRI is ordered to assess ligament injuries.

P:
I will order an MRI to assess ligament injuries. I will perform outpatient surgery tomorrow to align the bones and ligaments. You will be discharged home that evening. You will be in a cast for six to eight weeks and will not be able to weight-bear on the foot. You will advance your ambulation gradually based on how you heal and tolerate the procedure. I will see you again in twenty-four hours post-procedure and then again in two weeks. I will schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks. I will provide crutches for your use. S The patient reports a sore right foot, with intermittent swelling and tenderness. He reports a previous ankle injury twenty years ago that occasionally causes soreness. He also reports soreness in his other ankle and has had surgery on that ankle. He describes the initial foot injury as dropping a landscape brick on his foot, causing immediate pain and swelling. He rated his current pain as eleven out of ten. He reports numbness in the entire foot since the incident. He has been taking no medications for the pain. O Physical exam reveals bruising on the bottom and top of the right foot. Swelling is present. Capillary refill is brisk. Dorsalis pedis pulse is strong and bounding. Neurovascular exam is intact. X-ray shows dorsal displacement of the base of the second metatarsal with a three millimeter separation of the first and second metatarsal bases and presence of bony fragments. A The patient's right foot pain is due to a Lisfranc fracture. The x-ray findings indicate a dorsal displacement of the second metatarsal and separation of the first and second metatarsals. Given the bony fragments, surgery is likely indicated to restore proper alignment and prevent long-term complications such as arch loss, flatfoot, and arthritis. An MRI is ordered to assess ligament injuries. P
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right foot pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Elijah Reyes is a pleasant 45-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of right foot pain. The patient was referred by his primary care physician. He sustained an injury yesterday when he dropped a landscape brick on his right foot while doing yard work. He was able to get up and continue working after the injury. He rates his pain level as an 11 out of 10. The patient also reports numbness in his entire right foot, which has been present for a long time. The patient denies taking any medication for pain.

The patient reports he fractured his right ankle 20 years ago. He received non-operative treatment with casting. He has experienced intermittent soreness and swelling in his right ankle since then.

The patient reports surgical history of his left ankle. He continues to experience soreness and occasional giving way of the left ankle.

MEDICAL HISTORY

Patient reports history of a right ankle fracture 20 years ago.

SURGICAL HISTORY

Patient reports history of left ankle surgery.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right foot pain, right ankle soreness and swelling, and left ankle soreness and instability.
Neurological: Reports right foot numbness.

VITALS

All vital signs are within the normal limits.

PHYSICAL EXAM

CV: Capillary refill is brisk in less than 3 seconds in the right foot. Strong bounding dorsalis pedis pulse.
NEURO: Normal sensation. Right foot motor and sensation are intact and equal to the contralateral side.
MSK: Examination of the right foot: Bruising on the plantar and dorsal aspects of the foot. I do appreciate associated swelling. Tenderness to palpation over the midfoot.

RESULTS

An x-ray of the right foot was obtained and reviewed today. It demonstrates dorsal displacement of the base of the 2nd metatarsal with a 3 mm separation of the 1st and 2nd metatarsal bases and presence of bony fragments.

ASSESSMENT

Right foot pain, due to a Lisfranc fracture.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with him regarding his current symptoms. We discussed treatment options and I have recommended that we proceed with a right foot ORIF and all indicated procedures. We reviewed the risks, benefits, and alternatives of the surgery. I advised him that this procedure will be performed in an outpatient setting and he will be discharged home that same evening. He will then follow up with me 24 hours post procedure, and again 2 weeks later. I explained that he will be placed in a cast and will remain non-weight-bearing for 6 to 8 weeks. He will use crutches while ambulating and we will advance his weight-bearing gradually based on how he tolerates the procedure.

I have also recommended that we obtain an MRI of the right foot to further assess the ligaments. I explained to him that if he has poor bone alignment or ligament healing, this can lead to losing the arch in his foot causing pes planus and developing arthritis. I will send an order to the outpatient MRI facility downstairs for him to obtain the MRI this afternoon.

The patient wishes to think over his options before proceeding with the operation.

section_miscategorizations.text: An MRI is ordered to assess ligament injuries.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Ordering an MRI is a management action and belongs in the Plan, not in the Assessment.

section_miscategorizations.text: S The patient reports a sore right foot, with intermittent swelling and tenderness. He reports a previous ankle injury twenty years ago that occasionally causes soreness. He also reports soreness in his other ankle and has had surgery on that ankle. He describes the initial foot injury as dropping a landscape brick on his foot, causing immediate pain and swelling. He rated his current pain as eleven out of ten. He reports numbness in the entire foot since the incident. He has been taking no medications for the pain.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This entire block is subjective history and symptoms but is duplicated and appended to the Plan section, which could confuse the distinction between patient-reported information and management actions.

section_miscategorizations.text: O Physical exam reveals bruising on the bottom and top of the right foot. Swelling is present. Capillary refill is brisk. Dorsalis pedis pulse is strong and bounding. Neurovascular exam is intact. X-ray shows dorsal displacement of the base of the second metatarsal with a three millimeter separation of the first and second metatarsal bases and presence of bony fragments.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This block is objective exam and imaging data but is duplicated and appended to the Plan section, mixing findings with management actions.

section_miscategorizations.text: A The patient's right foot pain is due to a Lisfranc fracture. The x-ray findings indicate a dorsal displacement of the second metatarsal and separation of the first and second metatarsals. Given the bony fragments, surgery is likely indicated to restore proper alignment and prevent long-term complications such as arch loss, flatfoot, and arthritis. An MRI is ordered to assess ligament injuries.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This block is the diagnostic assessment and rationale but is duplicated and appended to the Plan section, conflating problem synthesis with treatment planning.
